Mostly I need a package that can handle non-linear functions. For example, here are the results for a nonlinear function of two random variables that are distributed according to a multi-variate normal. 

```python
x_mean = [1.0, 2.0]
x_covariance = [[1.0, 0.1], [0.1, 1.0]]

def f(x):
    return 1/(x[0]**2 + x[1]**2 + 1) + x[0]x[1]**2
```
The below results compare 5 different methods for computing the function. The most important is the Monte Carlo plot in purple since this can be considered the "true" value of the function. 
The blue is a linear theory method that utilizes the gradient of the function with respect to the two RVs. 
The next is a second order version of the same method that utilizes the Hessian in addition to the gradient. These two methods use a "black box" evaluation, making use of an automatic differentiation package to compute the gradient and Hessian. 
The result using the `uncertainties` package are in red. As you can see they are linear, as noted in the `uncertainties` documentation. 
My implementation is in cyan, and is almost exactly the same as the Monte Carlo results. It is also a transparent computation class in the same way as`Quantity`.

![uq_methods_comparison](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/17606949/14341994/806ed990-fcea-11e5-882a-53a3d9f293c6.png)

Of course these results vary for different functions and distributions of the rvs, but the important point is that `uncertainties` in its current state cannot compute nonlinear functions to the degree of accuracy that I would like.  Additionally, I didn't like that I needed to import `uncertainties.umath` for all of my math operations since I'm working with a large code base. I was able to write methods for the class such as `sin` so that `numpy.sin` calls them. Since I have to use `numpy` anyway, this made my imports and function writing a lot easier. Thus `numpy` is required, but you were probably going to be using it anyway if you were doing anything with random variables. My class is also slower, although still on the order of milliseconds at worst. You really can't tell over one function call.

So all in all I wanted more functionality and more accuracy than `uncertainties` could offer. Hope that helps.

I have to admit, functions that produce new `Literal` objects has always been a bit of a challenge.

The way the Tseitin transformation works is to just start with index 0 with an empty constraint list. Then just keep incrementing the index as you continue to append new constraints. Fortunately, it's not a nestable operation, so you don't have to worry about the problem you described.

Rather than using a global variable, I think you might want to try the approach of adding new name prefixes. Like at the top level start with `auxvarname = 'a'`, then at nesting level 1 using `b.a`, then `c.b.a`, and so forth. When you actually look at the expression, it will be nice to tell the variables apart at different nesting levels.

Did you upload StandardFirmata to your board? The sketch from the link above will not work with PyMata.

There are issues with performing a software reset on some models of the Arduino's. If you restart your python script, the Arduino may be unaware of this, even though PyMata sends a reset commend. As a result, the Arduino keeps streaming data (usually analog) and when the python script comes on line, it is receiving streaming data it does not expect..

If the time delay works, use it, or reset the board after stopping the Python script by pulling out the USB cable, and plugging it back in, waiting for the LEDs on the Arduino to stop blinking and then restart your script.

The pymata-aio library has some added features to prevent this. In FirmataPlus (not part of StandardFirmata), there is a way to set a keep-alive timer. What this does is, if the Arduino does not receive a keep-alive poll within a certain period of time (the time is settable by the user), it forces a hardware reset on the Arduino. So if you kill your Python script, the keep_alive polls will no longer be sent, and the FirmataPlus sketch forces the board to go into a hardware reset. Streaming data is killed and everyone is happy.

If you continue to have issues with your script, please provide a copy and I will take a look at it.

This PR tries to accomplish fixing the building in a way that is compatible with python wheels (see PEP-427: https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0427/). Wheels have become a very popular way to distribute complex python packages (see for example numpy/scipy/pandas/tensorflow/pytorch/scikit-learn who also distribute their package this way). It allows the user to install dlib from pypi in a matter of seconds instead of having to go through a compilation phase.

In order to be able to build a wheel one needs to precompile all assets, which is done on manylinux to be as widely compatible as possible.

Hello @aterrel,

There are a couple of issues with your example:

1. First, you created a new `ProvDocument` in the `doc` variable, but you used `iris_doc` in the second statement.
2. All namespace prefixes, i.e. `ex:` and `foaf:`, must be declared. They were not in your example.

The exception was raised because the library could not create a valid identifier for the element `e1` as it did not know what namespace `ex:` referred to.

I suggest that you look at the tutorial at http://trungdong.github.io/prov-python-short-tutorial.html for examples of namespace declarations (and more).

executable is required.

> I'm not arguing it's "wrong" per se, but it's one thing to fix an improper behavior in an
> existing method, but it's quite another to introduce an API change to the versions 
> where it's not expected in compliant interpreter.

This may not be expected or improper for `subprocess.Popen`, in which case I would agree with you, but here we're talking about `psutil.Popen`, which is a third-party wrapper exposing a tons of additional methods on top of `subproces.Popen` already. 
`__enter__` / `__exit__` is just yet another addition, and exposing it all the time will save the user the extra hassle deriving from checking the Python version, psutil version or whether `__enter__` is defined at runtime. 
`psutil.Popen` makes the promise it can always be used as a ctx manager no matter what the Python version is and that really is it.

IMO it is just more practical if the interface tries to expose the same things on all python versions as this is a principle which is already applied to all other psutil APIs. The fact that `psutil.Popen` is a wrapper on top of `subprocess.Popen` is not a good enough reason to break this rule.

To quote the Python Zen I would argue this is a case where practicality beats purity.

I don't expect anybody using `hasattr(psutil.Popen, "__enter__")` or `hasattr(subprocess.Popen, "__enter__")` to check the Python version at runtime.  I expect they would do that to check whether the object supports the `with` statement, not the Python version.

The dragtable plugin includes several callbacks, which sadly are not documented very well.

* `persistState` - url or function -> plug in your custom persistState function right here. function call is persistState(originalTable)

After our last discussion (issue #129) I was already planning to revisit whether the FFF Plugin CLI (when called with `calibre-debug --run-plugin FanFicFare`) can safely update the library and how much of the usual plugin capability might be possible now.  

But I really don't like the idea of executing `calibredb` from inside FanFicFare CLI.  I apologize for not making that clear during the issue #129 discussion.

It's expected for the GUI PI to manipulate the library contents.  But to do so by calling `calibredb` from the CLI--even more so the pip installed CLI--is, IMO, inappropriate.  

Searching, removing and re-adding via `calibredb` like you've done is very dangerous.  It will discard other formats, custom column values, user added tags, etc.  That's not something I want to support as it will definitely burn users.

The next question is then whether it's worthwhile to include the other changes for email fetch and 'output file' for cron retry in the FFF CLI directly vs a separate tool.  If included in FFF, I would be approaching them primarily as features targeted for non-Calibre users.  

If you'd like, I can link to your [AutomatedFanfic](https://github.com/MrTyton/AutomatedFanfic) tool from the FFF pages as a cron update solution for other users to look at.

FYI: The var `__version__` is in the latest committed `fanficfare/__init__.py` file, but not latest released.  If you don't have it, I suspect either you're not working from the very latest source, or your cli.py is importing from the installed version instead of your build area.

It's a tricky question. I think it should be started using an existing Bus instance, since some interfaces require a handle and even if they only require a channel name then that could be read from the Bus instance. I guess usually you would like to both send and receive messages.

We could have both an API with a CyclicTask class where you pass a Bus instance as argument and a ``bus.send_periodic()`` method which could be a short-hand for creating that class.

I started the thread based fallback but I'm a bit unsure how to prevent calling the ``send()`` method simultaneously from different threads if it is used by both cyclic send tasks and for regular sending. Right now each interface would have to protect their send function with locks.

### Socketcan ###
Uses channel name when configuring BCM. Bitrate is determined when setting up the link externally.

### Kvaser ###
Has "object buffers" for sending periodically. Requires a handle to configure it.

### NI-CAN ###
Has "CAN objects" for sending periodically. Can be configured with a channel name but probably requires the CAN-bus to be configured beforehand with correct bitrate etc.

### Remote ###
Would benefit from re-using an existing bus connection instead of creating one connection for each task.

Hi. This may be or may not be a limitation of `backtrader`, depending on your expectations.

For having multiple datas, the platform needs that the datas are time aligned and obviously *instruments* with different trading calendars are not time aligned.

There is a good reason for it. See the following naive `next` implementation for a strategy with two such datas:

```python
def next(self):
    if self.data0.close[0] > self.data1.close[0]:
        self.buy()
```

And we have a trading day in which the `^GSPC` (*data0*) traded and the the `^GDAXI` (*data1*) didn't.

  - What should be the value of `self.data1.close[0]`?

The platform has the notion of a `data master` which is the 1st data added to the system. The other datas have to remain in sync with it and the design pattern was made to make it possible to support multiple timeframes (mixing for example *daily* and *weekly* bars)

Looking at the data for `^GSPC` and `^GDAXI` in 2012 the 1st thing:

  - `^GSPC` traded `250` days
  - `^GDAXI` traded `256` days

Let's see what happens if we check the dates for equality with `^GSPC` as *data0* and `^GDAXI` as *data1*:

```
0001: True: data0 2012-01-03T23:59:59 : 2012-01-03T23:59:59 data1
0002: True: data0 2012-01-04T23:59:59 : 2012-01-04T23:59:59 data1
0003: True: data0 2012-01-05T23:59:59 : 2012-01-05T23:59:59 data1
0004: True: data0 2012-01-06T23:59:59 : 2012-01-06T23:59:59 data1
0005: True: data0 2012-01-09T23:59:59 : 2012-01-09T23:59:59 data1
0006: True: data0 2012-01-10T23:59:59 : 2012-01-10T23:59:59 data1
0007: True: data0 2012-01-11T23:59:59 : 2012-01-11T23:59:59 data1
0008: True: data0 2012-01-12T23:59:59 : 2012-01-12T23:59:59 data1
0009: True: data0 2012-01-13T23:59:59 : 2012-01-13T23:59:59 data1
0010: False: data0 2012-01-17T23:59:59 : 2012-01-16T23:59:59 data1
...
...
0248: False: data0 2012-12-27T23:59:59 : 2012-12-18T23:59:59 data1
0249: False: data0 2012-12-28T23:59:59 : 2012-12-19T23:59:59 data1
0250: False: data0 2012-12-31T23:59:59 : 2012-12-20T23:59:59 data1
```

Already on 2012-01-17 (or 16 for `^GDAXI`) there is a discrepancy. But looking at the final result it is clear that `^GSPC` is the `datamaster` because its `250` bars have been delivered. The platform has made its best attempt to deliver the next best possible bar for `^GDAXI` but there are 6 more and cannot keep up.

If we reverse the roles and make `^GDAXI` to be *data0*

```
0001: True: data0 2012-01-03T23:59:59 : 2012-01-03T23:59:59 data1
0002: True: data0 2012-01-04T23:59:59 : 2012-01-04T23:59:59 data1
0003: True: data0 2012-01-05T23:59:59 : 2012-01-05T23:59:59 data1
0004: True: data0 2012-01-06T23:59:59 : 2012-01-06T23:59:59 data1
0005: True: data0 2012-01-09T23:59:59 : 2012-01-09T23:59:59 data1
0006: True: data0 2012-01-10T23:59:59 : 2012-01-10T23:59:59 data1
0007: True: data0 2012-01-11T23:59:59 : 2012-01-11T23:59:59 data1
0008: True: data0 2012-01-12T23:59:59 : 2012-01-12T23:59:59 data1
0009: True: data0 2012-01-13T23:59:59 : 2012-01-13T23:59:59 data1
0010: False: data0 2012-01-16T23:59:59 : 2012-01-13T23:59:59 data1
...
...
0254: True: data0 2012-12-27T23:59:59 : 2012-12-27T23:59:59 data1
0255: True: data0 2012-12-28T23:59:59 : 2012-12-28T23:59:59 data1
0256: True: data0 2012-12-31T23:59:59 : 2012-12-31T23:59:59 data1
```

The `256` trading bars of  `^GDAXI` have been delivered and the platform has tried to compensate the lack of bars in `^GSPC` by taking the next best bar. See how at indexes `0009` and `0010` the date `2015-01-13` repeats and this is because the `data slave` (`^GSPC`) cannot overtake the `datamaster` (`^GDAXI`)

The solution would be to add bars to make both of them have the same length and therefore be time aligned. But then a fundamental question shows up which has to be answered by the one looking for results and not by the platform:

  - What values (ohlc and volume for example) must this *gap* take?

And the answer is not trivial:

  - The bar may be empty (filled with `float('NaN')`) and then calculation of indicators would break with the `NaN` values.

    No, indicators cannot be looking at each individual bar to see if this is a *gap* bar or not, because they are meant to be simple mathematical formulas looking at the `x` past delivered values.

  - The bar may take the values of the last seen bar (or a valued based on those seen)

    The indicators remain active but deliver slightly different results than on the original trading days.

`backtrader` lets you add bars. See this post for how to do it:

  - http://www.backtrader.com/posts/2015-11-21-data-filters/data-filling-filtering/

A `CalendarDays` filter exists which adds bars (choosing how to) for the missing trading days and a sample in the sources:

  - https://github.com/mementum/backtrader/tree/master/samples/calendar-days

This is not strictly speaking a bug. For a dictionary, Voluptuous will attempt to match each key against all keys in the schema. If multiple keys fail, which error should it report?

For example, in the following schema, there are two possible keys that could report errors. Voluptuous does not know which one to report, so it reports the most generic error.

```python
>>> s = Schema({All(str, Coerce(int)): 1, 'bar': int})
>>> s({'foo': 1})
...
MultipleInvalid: extra keys not allowed @ data['asd']
```

In your specific case where there is only one key it would be possible to report a deeper error. But it's arguable whether that error "more" correct than "extra keys not allowed".

I do understand that this is perhaps not what one would expect, but if you can propose a general solution that works well in all cases I'd be happy to consider it.

I've made a start on this, beginning with the transit server (since it's already possible to run `wormhole-server start` without a `--transit=` argument, disabling the transit server but still running the rendezvous server). The new repo is in https://github.com/warner/magic-wormhole-transit-relay . That repo kinda works, but I'm currently blocked on making the first release by two items:

* test coverage: Some of the transit server code was only exercised by running the actual client against it, which happens in the wormhole client unit tests.
* usage/status notification: I'm going back and forth about how I want the transit server to report metrics. The server in this repo writes blurred usage data to a sqlite database, and also writes a "current status report" to a JSON file every 5 minutes (which the Munin plugin then reads). @exarkun has me half-convinced to just write everything to stdout instead, and let the server admin decide what to do with the data. For my own server instance, I'll have to write a tool that connects to stdout and writes stuff into the same sqlite DB and current-status-report.

The plan is to get an initial release of magic-wormhole-transit-relay made, then change this repo to 1: add a dev/testing dependency on magic-wormhole-transit-relay, 2: change the unit tests to import that code when building a test server, and finally 3: delete the transit code from this repo (removing the `--transit` argument from `wormhole-server start` in the process).

Then to migrate things, I'll do:

* set up a new host
* install the transit-relay on it
* remove `--transit=` from the old server, restart it, and start a port-forwarder from old host to the new IP address
* change DNS to point at the new one
* wait for it to propagate, wait for all existing connections to complete, then turn off the forwarder
* at some point, update the old server to a version that doesn't have the transit relay

Later, I plan to do the same process with the rendezvous server code.

I think the problem could be that the PayPal sandbox has no proper support for TLS and the requests library has been updated to not support the downgrade of the SSL protocol to versions that are not safe. For example, to receive the IPN notifications in my server I had setup stunnel ( https://www.stunnel.org/index.html ) , and I had configure it to allow all versions of SSL with this config file:
```
pid=
cert = stunnel.pem
sslVersion = all
foreground = yes
output = stunnel.log
debug=7
options=-NO_SSLv3
options=-NO_SSLv2

[https]
accept=8443
connect=8001
TIMEOUTclose=60
```

The key part there is the options  -NO_SSLv3 .

I guess, that in PayPal Pro the problem is that is our server the one making requests (so, setting up a proxy for incoming connections will not solve the problem), and our library not supporting the protocol downgrade. 

Here is an article about using requests allowing other SSL versions : https://lukasa.co.uk/2013/01/Choosing_SSL_Version_In_Requests/ , but .. that would mean that we have to modify the code.

So, my proposed solution is : 
modify your /etc/hosts file, to resolve the PayPal domain name to a local proxy ip (I don't know if you can use stunnel for this purpose), and configure the proxy to support all SSL protocols.

First, a bit of context on this pull request: is there any way that Conda can pull the version, description, and dependency information from the `setup.py` file? If I merge this pull request, then it looks like I am doomed forever to have to make every change to the project metadata in two places instead of in one place. (Or, just as likely, to forget to make the change in the Conda files and then the two will be out of sync.)

I do see that the Conda file describing how to run the tests is telling Conda something that is not included in the `setup.py` — but is there some way to avoid duplicating all the rest of it? I'm a bit surprised, given how ubiquitous `setup.py` files are, that Conda doesn’t have a switch that says “pull all of this usual stuff from the `setup.py`” — or that Conda doesn't at least have a packager that could take the `.tar.gz` that results from `setup.py sdist` and use its `PKG-INFO` file to get all of the information that it needs (since there it's in declarative, rather than executable, form).

If Conda lakes this ability, I'd almost rather write a tool for them that can turn a Python `.tar.gz` into a Conda package, than have to maintain this whole set of extra text files separately from where I'm already keeping up with this information. (Especially since I maintain several projects and if something does not stop the madness, they soon will all have these files, each repeating exactly what's already in their `setup.py` — so I'm asking because it does not just involve this one package, but a dozen that I maintain!)

@zedrdave Here's what I've found. At least what I understand. I might be wrong.

**Django-rest** came first, custom/integrated schemas, arg parsing, UI interface, etc. Class-based endpoint handling. All integrated with Django, and all that that means, like:
* you are tied to a relational database
* if you use Celery and want to use the DB models, you have to install the full stack everywhere, you couldn't very easily install and use just SQLAlchemy without the full Django stack
* You define routes with regex in a specific location separated from the actual endpoints (although I understand that changed in the latest Django)

**Flask-RESTful** came afterwards, to be a sort-of Flask copy of Django-rest, working very similarly. It is probably the most popular in GitHub stars because it came first. But as far as I could investigate, its development kind of stagnated. Custom/integrated schemas, arg parsing, UI interface, etc. Class-based endpoint handling, etc.

**Flask-RESTplus** started as a fork of Flask-RESTful, to keep improving on what was there. Custom/integrated schemas, arg parsing, but exposing a Swagger endpoint, so it can use better UI (Swagger UI) and integration with other things.

**Flask-apispec**, which is what I'm using, is maintained by the same guys of Webargs and Marshmallow, @jmcarp and @sloria . In my opinion, it is probably the most under-rated Flask plug-in and one of the best (with Webargs and Marshmallow). I think it deserves way more attention than it has, certainly more than my own open source Flask projects. It does something very simple, it just ties Webargs and Marshmallow with the Flask routes internally using Apispec, generating the swagger endpoint (live docs) automatically. I think it works wonderfully. The problem is maybe that the docs are not as complete as all the alternatives. But most of the docs you really need are actually from Webargs and Marshmallow. You can use simple Flask routes or class-based routes.

Because it uses Webargs:

* You get to parse even nested arguments, you could specify that a deeply nested field in a JSON document should have a specific value, is required etc, and it will just work. 

You can specify that a JSON field `people` should be a list of dicts with names in which the `last_name` is required. Something like:

```json
{
  "people": [
    {"first_name": "John", "last_name": "Doe"},
    {"first_name": "Johny", "last_name": "Doey"}
  ]
}
```

* You get your arg parsed and converted or the client user gets a very specific error telling him exactly where is his error. And it's all documented and with an automatic UI that documents the API and let's you explore it with Swagger UI.

Because it uses Marshmallow:

You can declare models that can reference each other (for example if you use a relational DB) and you can specify that the response should contain only some fields, and should contain only up to some level of nested objects, but without some fields, etc. And again, it gets auto documented in the Swagger endpoint that you can explore with Swagger UI or anything compatible. Right now I don't remember the specific advantages of Marshmallow vs custom serializers, but I remember it is quite more powerful, I think that the nesting of models is not very easy to do with custom serializers.

---

About my project generator, it just generates the very simple basic code to get up and running with Flask-apispec, with Webargs, Marshmallow, authentication with JWT tokens, SQLAlchemy models with migrations, but not using Flask-SQLAlchemy to be able to switch easily and/or use the same models in Celery workers (For example, I have some projects where I modified those bits to use CouchDB and some with ElasticSearch).

It also generates code to start working with Celery workers connected to the same app, simplified API testing with pytest, everything tied together with Docker, with easy local development and deployment with Docker Swarm mode, CI ready, with the base to create a frontend container where you can build an Angular/React/whateverjs front end app, that shares the same URL as your Flask API, handled by Traefik proxy with automatic HTTPS certificates generation.

If you have a public Linux server you can get it up and running, starting from scratch, with about 5 commands. Install Docker, generate the project, create the first migration, deploy. And there you can test it and discard it right away if you want.

BTW, with the way paths are handled by Traefik, you could have your standard API in one endpoint (say `https://example.com/api/v1/`) and some parts, or a new API in other endpoint (say `https://example.com/api/v2/`), being it with Flask-apispec or whatever you decide to use.

I'm probably missing some stuff, but that's basically all my [`full-stack`](https://github.com/tiangolo/full-stack) project does, it's sort of simple. It is NOT a new framework, just a starter project generator using the tools and setup I've found works best for me and my teams over time.

If I wanted to have access to the user I would override this method and store the user id (once again, websockets are meant to be lightweight and fast, store a little as you can)

Something along the lines of:
```python
    user_id = None

    def set_pubsub_channels(self, request, channels):
        super(MySubscriber, self).set_pubsub_channels(request, channels)
        try:
            this.user_id = request.user.id
        except AttributeError:
            # Once again if anything were to happen inside your code, t would prevent the connection to work
            # so make sure to catch everything. 
            pass

Actually, with regard to option `3`, that only solves the case where someone tries to publish on multiple threads. If they do any other operation, that too could result in a frame error. So the only way to really make that work well is to grab a lock inside every method that wants to call `send_frame`, which I can see quickly leading to a lot of bugs (e.g. synchronous transport, `try/finally` debugging, etc) and likely seriously slow things down, which in turn means I think most users would want the option to control that themselves, leading to even more complications.

I'm leaning in the direction of a helper such as `ChannelPool` and documentation. An important but subtle detail such as this could easily be lost in documentation though.

When using the wildcard, you're implicitly copying multiple files (e.g. `foo/*` means all files that start with `foo/`). The `destination_object` doesn't make sense in that context, so it was used to specify an extra prefix (often called a "folder" or "subfolder", although GCS, like S3, doesn't have an inherent hierarchical organisation) to be prepended to the destination paths.

Here's an example: let's assume that we have two files in a bucket called `some_source_bucket`: `foo/file1.txt` and `foo/file2.txt`. Now, say that we do:

```
copy_foo_files = GoogleCloudStorageToGoogleCloudStorageOperator(
    dag=dag,
    task_id='copy_foo_files',
    source_bucket='some_source_bucket',
    destination_bucket='some_destination_bucket',
    source_object='foo/*',
    destination_object='bar/baz'
)
```

This will copy files:

```
gs://some_source_bucket/foo/file1.txt
gs://some_source_bucket/foo/file2.txt
```
into:
```
gs://some_destination_bucket/bar/baz/foo/file1.txt
gs://some_destination_bucket/bar/baz/foo/file2.txt
```

Exactly. The external system generating alerts for Alerta should just forward alerts with the appropriate severity and leave Alerta to decide what the alert status should be based on what it currently knows about an alert.

It is possible to manually close alerts but it is discouraged. Alerts should auto-close based on the root cause being fixed and the alert changing to "normal" or "ok". There is the ability to close alerts manually for cases where alerts don't have a corresponding "normal" severity.

For example, log files are common sources of error messages where there's no obvious way to auto-close them (except by timing them out or manually closing them).

In terms of assimp's support of glTF 2.0: much of the core parts of the spec are implemented: 

- asset
- buffers
   - except GLB - see below
- bufferViews
- scenes / scene
- nodes and meshes
- images
   - both via external uri and embedded base64-encoded data uris
   - unsure about referencing via bufferViews. I think it's in there
- textures
- materials
   - the basic properties, pbrMetallicRoughness and the pbrSpecularGlossiness extension are fully implemented.
- accessors, sort of - see next section
- I'm unsure about cameras support. I think it's in there.

What remains to be implemented/fixed is this functionality:

-  binary_glTF†
   - I started work on it [here a while ago,](https://github.com/Matter-and-Form/assimp/tree/feature/gltf2-binary) but I've not gotten far.
- animations/joints/skins/morph targets (and by extension, their accessors)
   - assimp currently outputs some data for these, but it's copied from assimp's glTF 1.0 implementation, so it's incorrect
- sparse accessors 

I didn't get around to fixing these, partly due to it not being a priority for my use cases, and partly because I'm unfamiliar with it.

Persisting `name` properties is a bit inconsistent, and `extras` properties also should be serialized and persisted.

---

glTF 2.0 extension implementations that are on the road map include:

- KHR_material_common/KHR_materials_cmnBlinnPhong
- lights
- Draco compression (to replace the now removed Open3DGC compression)

I think we'll wait until the extension specs are finalized before implementing these.

Speaking of KHR_material_common: several of us (in #1423) were unclear as to the state of KHR_material_common (or its equivalent) for glTF 2.0, partly due to to scattering of issues/PRs that address it, and the moving-target/ work-in-progress implementations in other loaders (namely three.js). As I understand it, the current plan is to implement a "common material" via KHR_materials_cmnBlinnPhong, yes? Is this the PR that we should be following:  [KhronosGroup/glTF#1075](https://github.com/KhronosGroup/glTF/pull/1075#issuecomment-328408375)?

---

As for further help, it'd be appreciated if Khronos was able to verify the correctness of the export functionality (and import too, for that matter) of glTF 2 models. I tested against most of the models from the Khronos sample repo, and most pass the glTF validator with flying colours. The ones with animations/joints/skins/morphTargets present many errors.

@jdavid thank you for your response

> libgit2 uses the same license, and I think that's the reason pygit2 uses this license. It was that way when I took maintenance of the project, and I actually do like the copyleft property.

As the maintainer I hope you are aware of the implications of libgit2's license on pygit2.

Does pygit2 actually mix / share source with libgit2 or git in any way?  Or just link to it?

If it actually borrows code from libgit2 or git, license change wouldn't work because it'd be a derivitive no matter what.

As it stands, the [ruby plugin](https://github.com/libgit2/rugged) is [licensed MIT](https://github.com/libgit2/rugged/blob/master/LICENSE).

> I actually do like the copyleft property.

I never doubled the good intention of copyleft and those who extol it. The problem is in contemporary times we're beginning to find the benefits that copyleft assures us don't need to be put to paper.  The virtue of the project and its contributor's being open source leads to the code being available and accessible for the sake of convenience. Contributions end up coming back because its burdensome to maintain internal forks, when a proprietary project dev's does the numbers, it almost always results in them benefitting from contributing back a patch so the community share the responsibility maintaining it.

> The linking exception allows to be used the way it is intended to from proprietary software.

It seems to be well-engrained in the marketing pitch of libgit2. Its executed very well. From viewing the libgit homepage with the company logos for Microsoft, GH, etc (it must be commercial friendly) and "permissive licensing". libgit2's README says its under a **very permissive license** (emphasis libgit2's) . I'm sorry this isn't true.

The linking exception is a paragraph. Its stapled on top of 2000+ words of legalese that could have very strong implications to the developer. It doesn't say under X and Y circumstances, "it may not be used".

Rather, it cover broad circumstances, "You must cause any work that you distribute or publish, that in whole or in part contains or is derived from the Program or any part thereof," with potentially punitive ramifications, even to open source projects, "to be licensed as a whole at no charge to all third parties under the terms of this License." 

With pygit2's license, there is an exception to the broad circumstance, "unlimited permission to link the compiled version of this library into combinations with other programs, and to distribute those combinations without any restriction coming from the use of this file". Who in the python community uses the term "linking" and "compiling"? 

So if I put pygit2 on Android Store with Kivy... I can't use buildozer with .py files, it must be pyc.  What if an intermediary compiler like jython/pypy/pyston/cython/any other non-cpython implementation/my own build scripts alters the pygit2's code before its "compiled"?

Even with my ambitions are an open source project, I don't want to risk exposing my downstream users to licenses terms.

That is a foggier area, since python is used in a practical sense differently than C/C++ where we often see GPL used. I go into some points over my concerns with (L)GPL / GPL with linking exceptions at https://github.com/urwid/urwid/issues/41#issuecomment-30979846.

> A change is still possible, but it would require a large consensus from pygit2 contributors. And I have never heard the opinion on this matter by other contributors. So the change is unlikely to happen...

I have a lot of gratitude to you and the contributors. I think the intention is clear they want the library to useable to many as possible.

- mapbox dataset put-features: insert or update a set of features, performing as many backend requests as needed to get through the feature set
- mapbox dataset delete-features: delete a set of features, performing as many backend requests as needed to get through the id set
- mapbox dataset replace-dataset: list the ids in the dataset, delete them all and/or replace them with the features provided

Hidden here is a more generic question about how we want to handle "higher-level" commands that might result in multiple API requests.

@mlowicki this is a great bug report, thanks.  Sorry we haven't gotten to it yet.  I am on vacation at the moment, so perhaps post another ping next week :).

> 2.  I'm not super happy about storing the failures and having to test that, but I don't think there's any other way, since `Resource.render` will eat any other exception and just return a 500, and getting around that is pretty hard.  Any suggestions?

I think that burdening the caller with performing this check themselves every time is indeed the wrong thing to do: anywhere you say "and then the caller must..." is an opportunity for a mistake to be made.

What do you think of this, instead: require a `TestCase` as an argument to `StringStubbingResource`, and then instead of doing `self.test_case.fail(...)`, do `self.test_case.addCleanup(lambda: self.test_case.fail)`?  This puts the test failure in a context that reports directly to `TestCase` and doesn't get caught by intermediary resource-rendering code.

You can pass the `TestCase` along either to `StringStubbingResource` or to `SequenceStringStubs`.  In either case you should be able to eliminate the `failures` list; if you pass it to `StringStubbingResource` you can get rid of it entirely, if you pass it to `SequenceStringStubs`, you can replace it with a simpler `addFailure` reporting protocol; no need to couple to all the methods of `list` in the interface.

I made some time to dig into this, and I think there are actually two issues here. 

The first one is "when a branch route returns `None`, it's a `200 OK` response instead of a `404 NOT FOUND`." I actually disagree that it should 404. The way I read it is, `branch=True` is like tacking `*args, **kwargs` onto the end of a function's arg list: it's saying "add as much you want here, I can handle it." So when you have a `@route("/something", branch=True)` function, yup, it matches `GET /something/else` and lets the function do whatever it does. Returning `None` is a thing functions ever do. If the function never calls `request.setResponseCode`, the response goes with the default value of `t.w.http.Request.code`, which is `200 OK`.  And just like the way a function can return `None`, an HTTP server [can reply with an empty body](http://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7230.html#section-3.3):
> The presence of a message body in a request is signaled by a Content-Length
> or Transfer-Encoding header field. [...] The presence of a message body in a
> response depends on both the request method to which it is responding and the
> response status code. [...] All 1xx (Informational), 204 (No Content), and
> 304 (Not Modified) responses do not include a message body. All other
> responses do include a message body, although the body might be of zero
> length.

So the empty response is definitely counter-intuitive, and the server really should be replying with `204 NO CONTENT`, but it's legal. The idea that Klein might keep an eye out for endpoints that return `None` and twiddle the response codes of those to 404, 204, or anything else, gives me the heebie-jeebies pretty hard. That sounds like adding complexity whose payoff is small and which also creates a new behavior that is magic and is begging for trouble. To me, this falls firmly into the domain of "framework users are allowed to write bad code."

The other issue is that the behavior with branch routes and `strict_slashes` is _inconsistent._ I do think that this is a bug, and because `strict_slashes` controls Werkzeug behavior while `branch` controls Twisted behavior, I'd confidently bet that they have some impedance mismatch that's causing that bug. I'll sniff around a little at that one, but I recommend that we not change the "if your endpoint returns `None` and doesn't set a non-200 response code, the server's response will be a `200 OK`" behavior.

It's a big diff, I know, but hopefully it's fairly straightforward. As far as tests go, I didn't modify the existing tests anymore than necessary to get them to run under Python3. I also added a bunch for the proxy/wrapping behaviour that wasn't covered before (which does expose some interesting quirks in the C implementation that I've faithfully copied).

As far as the implementation goes, I know it's not exactly idiomatic Python, but it's mostly a simple port of the existing C code. I'm hoping this helps ease maintenance of the parallel implementations, as well as catching any corner cases still not covered by tests. 

One of the most interesting or controversial quirks is the behaviour of the C proxy wrappers when it comes to comparisons. Specifically, it only delegates to `__cmp__`, which is not really applicable in Python 3. On the principle of least surprise for upgraders, I kept that behaviour intact across all platforms (I can imagine breakage if `__eq__` and friends started getting called suddenly sometimes, if they're ever even implemented). Maybe there's something better?

I currently only know the Python C API well enough to read it, but if you want me to take a shot at porting the C extension to Python 3 I can give that a try.

I have one remaining quibble, and that’s the “<3.7” in python_requires. Is that really necessary? That is, do we *know* this project won’t work on 3.7, to the point you shouldn’t even install it? 

I would prefer to avoid that restriction for a few reasons. I don’t like adding Yet Another thing that has to be updated for every python release (tox, Travis, appveyor, classifiers, changenote).  It’s going to cause serious pain on day one of general availability of 3.7 for every project that hasn’t already rereleased if that becomes widespread. It took many months to get the projects that make up the ZTK to have releases claiming 3.6 support, and very few, if any, had any actual code changes that were needed to make that happen. 

tl;dr I feel like pining an upper bound in python_requires is more hassle than benefit, hurts early adopters, and violates the spirit of “consenting adults”

hmm, not really sure if i want to consider that slow, but i'd say that rdflib currently definitely isn't really optimized for speed (and neither are most RDF serialization formats in general). Python isn't the fastest wrt. string parsing, but i think the two main areas of speeding up parsing are the parsers themselves and the store implementations.

Creating special purpose parsers for example for the _canonical_ forms of ntriples, ttl, etc. (the canonical form greatly simplifies parsing and would make it possible to speed it up a lot) has always been on my todo list, but i didn't find the time yet.

Wrt. the stores it heavily depends on what backend you're using, the default (Integer Optimized Memory) not really being the most efficient in terms of size and parsing speed, but rather optimized for later queries.

That said: in the current master branch i optimized the hashing time for all terms (https://github.com/RDFLib/rdflib/commit/2fa4d8be9efb0842af5e82bf768c67fac00038e6), so maybe that is already slightly faster (you can try it out with `pip install https://github.com/RDFLib/rdflib/archive/master.zip`). Otherwise i'd currently suggest using a triple store with bulk import as a backend.

Except from that: contributions always welcome...

The `capture`-decorator is fine as it is. It's perfect to get proper error handling when capturing whole functions. What we want to be able to do is to capture arbitrary parts of code inside of functions as well, with the same error handling properties as the `capture`-decorator does provide. That's why we would like to see the same functionality as context manager as well, as decorators can only be applied to functions, while context managers can be used inside of functions.

The example for the context manager I included above is a bit silly as the only code inside the function, which is not going to be traced, is a print function, but just imagine more complicated code flow. We e.g. have the case that we have a single function in which we want to trace multiple parts of code separately, something not possible with a decorator.

So I just ran my "do-nothing" reproducible case (thanks to @dmontag) that reliably thrashes the CPU after 65 minutes. I can confirm that after installing a custom `grpcio` with https://github.com/grpc/grpc/pull/13665, the thrashing goes away!

I have created a `manylinux` 64-bit wheel that includes the backport: https://github.com/dhermes/google-cloud-pubsub-performance/blob/master/grpcio-1.7.4.dev1-cp36-cp36m-manylinux1_x86_64.whl

----

Feel free to install but "buyer beware" this isn't a wheel endorsed by the `grpcio` folks. The wheel was created via:

```
$ docker run \
>   --rm \
>   --tty \
>   --interactive \
>   --volume $(pwd):/var/wheels/ \
>   quay.io/pypa/manylinux1_x86_64:latest \
>   /bin/bash
% cd tmp/
% git clone https://github.com/dhermes/grpc
% cd grpc
% git checkout 1.7.3-with-13665
% git submodule update --init
% /opt/python/cp36-cp36m/bin/python3.6 -m pip install --upgrade pip wheel
% /opt/python/cp36-cp36m/bin/python3.6 -m pip install --requirement requirements.txt
% export REPO_ROOT=$(pwd)
% export GRPC_PYTHON_BUILD_WITH_CYTHON=1
% /opt/python/cp36-cp36m/bin/python3.6 -m pip wheel . --wheel-dir $(pwd)
% auditwheel repair grpcio-1.7.4.dev1-cp36-cp36m-linux_x86_64.whl --wheel-dir /var/wheels/
```

All of these commands will work on your target machine except for `auditwheel`, but if you build a custom wheel on your target machine (i.e. a wheel not intended for "general distribution") then you don't need to use `auditwheel`. Also note you'll need to change the relevant Python path from `/opt/python/cp36-cp36m/bin/python3.6`.

Can you try changing your view?

```python
# Using the 0.8 syntax while running 0.9
from datatableview.views.legacy import LegacyConfigurationDatatableView

class PresentationChangesDatatableView(LegacyConfigurationDatatableView):
    # ...
```

In version 0.9 and later, ``DatatableView`` looks for the new syntax, which you can use:

```python
# Using the new 0.9 syntax
from datatableview.views import DatatableView
from datatableview import Datatable, columns

list_values = [10, 1, 3, 20, 100, 15, 40, 50, 55]

# New 'Datatable' class looks a lot like a Django 'ModelForm'
class MyTable(Datatable):
    value = columns.IntegerColumn("VALUE", sources=['value'], processor='get_value')

    class Meta:
        columns = ['id', 'name', 'status', 'value']

    def get_value(self, instance, **kwargs):
        if instance.id < len(list_values):
            return list_values[instance.id]
        else:
            return 0


# The the view to use MyTable
class MyView(DatatableView):
    model = Task
    datatable_class = MyTable
    template_name = 'main.html'
```

Both of these examples should work for you.  Sorry for the changes!  Check out the example project included in the code for the updated live demos.

I think `RoleBasedPermission` is a fine high-level distinction to make, but my sense is that it could be further subdivided into a `UserBasedPermissions` and a `CollectionPermissions` before being subdivided again. The class as it stands has a split-personality for checking permissions based on the `target['type']`. [Here's a diagram](https://docs.google.com/a/learningequality.org/drawings/d/1BPku9fjNJicqPhdmidBDoQCCahPD5tLLiVaSD-8VBBo/edit?usp=sharing).

Along the same lines suggest splitting `Role.filter_by_relationship` into *two* methods: `Role.user_to_user` and `Role.user_to_collection`, which each separately filters by the indicated relationships. It is not more convenient to have one dual-purpose method.

> Adds a deep selector `>>>.buttons` (and one for the last button in this div) to apply consistent layout (right aligned, and flushed with right edge of modal contents)

This is the intended behavior ; `stream=False` returns a binary string containing all logs for the container once they've been retrieved. `stream=True` returns a generator that iterates over log data as it is received. As a result, the correct way to use `logs` with `stream=False` is thus:

```python
err_logs = container.logs(stdout=False, stderr=True, stream=False)
print(err_logs.decode('utf-8'))
```

While running through #2898, I found that this is a limitation that we brought upon ourselves with our queryset and how we model our API endpoints.

In our AppViewSet, we set `.get_queryset()` to give us only the applications that we have either created (i.e. we are the owner) or ones which we were given permission to use (`use_app` from django-guardian). This is such that when we list apps via `/v1/apps`, it will only show us that filtered viewset. However, that also limits the scope on what applications we can act upon. Since `.get_queryset()` returns a queryset of applications that we only know about, it returns a 404 when we try to "ping" an application we were not given access to.

Therefore, there are a few choices:

1. change `.get_queryset()` to be unbounded, manually patch `.list()` to show the limited queryset (ugly, but backwards-compatible)

2. change `.get_queryset()` to be unbounded, add a filter to `/v1/apps` to show apps relevant to the user, modify `deis apps:list` in the client to use this new endpoint (backwards-incompatible, but RESTful)

3. close this as a limitation and define as "working as intended".

(1) Would be the quickest solution, but ultimately means that we are manually patching one of our endpoints and cannot take advantage of future DRF fixes to `.list()`. (2) would be "the right thing" to do, but we're unable to break endpoint APIs any more now that we're considered backwards-compatible. Older clients would also break in that they'd be displaying all applications on the platform rather than the user's limited queryset of applications.

Hi!

> 1) Would deis be suitable for my use case in my production?

We are considered production-ready, so I don't see why not!

>  Where would the PostgreSQL database fit in the picture?

At the moment we have an open enhancement to the project for attaching resources to applications in #231. Until that is solved, you'll have to stand up your own database outside of Deis and [attach the application to the database](http://docs.deis.io/en/latest/using_deis/config-application/#attach-to-backing-services) through credentials stored in the config.

> Is this being considered for the future?

Docker compose specifies container links, exposed ports etc. whereas Deis sets up the exposed port address in the ephemeral range, and does not work with links (links only work across a single host). I think at some point we *may* have something similar to `docker-compose.yml` but it'll look a little different. It's more likely that it'll look closer to Cloud Foundry's [Application Manifest](http://docs.cloudfoundry.org/devguide/deploy-apps/manifest.html).

Hope this helps!

This is the expected behavior.  The countdown only happens if you specify `--timestring` _without_ other time-identifying flags.  See #348 and #349 for more information.  This edge case is documented [here](https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/client/curator/current/timestring.html):

> Starting in v3.1.0, if you specify `--timestring` without `--older-than` or `--newer-than`, the process will continue, but with a message warning you that the operation will delete all indices with a time string, followed by a 10 second countdown timer, which provides the opportunity to cancel the operation.

```
docs = es_helpers.scan(
    sourceCluster,
    index=args.sourceIndex,
    scroll=args.scroll,
    fields=('_source', '_parent', '_routing', '_timestamp')
)

totalCount = 0
batchCount = 0
batchSize = 100000
batchTime = time()

for d in docs:
    batchCount += 1
    totalCount += 1
    if batchCount == batchSize:
        print "Scanned", format(totalCount, ",d"), "docs (batch time:", _timeDiff(batchTime), \
            "/ total time:", _timeDiff(start), ")"
        batchCount = 0
        batchTime = time()

def _timeDiff(start):
    m, s = divmod(time() - start, 60)
    h, m = divmod(m, 60)
    return "%d:%02d:%02d" % (h, m, s)
```

## What I've learned

#### To analyze the error...

Use Linux and Python 2 and run the following test.

```py.test nbgrader/tests/apps/test_nbgrader_zip_collect.py```

---

#### Difference between Unicode and utf-8

> "Unicode only define code points, that is, a number which represents a character. How you store these code points in memory depends of the encoding that you are using. UTF-8 is one way of encoding Unicode characters, among many others." - https://stackoverflow.com/a/643719/2220152

---

#### What `# coding: utf-8` and `# -*- coding: utf-8 -*-` is about

> Ideally, you’d want to be able to write literals in your language’s natural encoding. You could then edit Python source code with your favorite editor which would display the accented characters naturally, and have the right characters used at runtime.
>
> Python supports writing Unicode literals in any encoding, but you have to declare the encoding being used. This is done by including a special comment as either the first or second line of the source file:
>
> ```# coding: utf-8```
>
> "The -*- symbols indicate to Emacs that the comment is special; they have no significance to Python but are a convention." - https://docs.python.org/2/howto/unicode.html#unicode-literals-in-python-source-code

#### nbconvert project

nbconvert seem to have run into similar issues. Look at [this io-helper](https://github.com/jupyter/nbconvert/blob/68a3925efef5c304c2c8682ad05cac9560c4173a/nbconvert/utils/io.py) they made.

Here are a couple of options:

1. Queue up the pending cells at the notebook level and run them when the kernel is set. This doesn't prevent the cells from being modified before they are run, though.

2. Make a fake "kernel" that just queues up execution requests, and set that to the cells when they are first created. This has the advantage that the cell logic identifying it's running, etc., still works as-is.

3. Throw an error when trying to run a cell and a kernel is not active.

I think a combination of (2) and (3) would work well.  The initial dummy kernel optimistically assumes there will eventually be a good connection, and just queues up execution requests.  However, if there is an error with the kernel, we throw an error to the user when trying to execute the cells.

> wrote:
>
>> the purpose of objects implementing the mapping interface is so people
>> can write functions that work on dict's, and if you happen to have an
>> instance instead, you can use the same function.
>>
>> Fair enough! The trouble is, right now the keys() etc. methods are not
>> safe to use. They're not even used by Schematics itself because they would
>> fail on any model that has a field with an overlapping name.
>>
>> So, if the methods are going to stay, then I think we should
>>
>>    - explicitly forbid any field names that would cause a conflict
>>    - accurately define the iterators' semantics; e.g., should
>>    serializables be included.
>>
>>
> This seems like a solid idea to me. The list of fields is likely very
> short and can be checked in the metaclass, meaning the overhead is
> negligible.
>
>
>>
>>
>> In my days of building Brubeck (the web framework), the difference
>> between using objects or functions for routing turned out to be roughly 33%
>> gains when using just functions.
>>
>> You mean a scenario involving Schematics? If you still have the testcase,
>> I'd love to get it for benchmarking.
>>
> First, this story is about Brubeck, not Schematics, but it helps you
> understand why I prefer not using classes when I have the choice and why I
> held it as a virtue of Schematics.
>
> The story is that while building Brubeck, I initially took the class
> approach to handlers that Tornado took. I then heard from Flask users that
> they liked just putting a decorator on functions, so I added support for
> that too.
>
> Because everything else was the same, I was able to directly test the
> performance of Tornado's class style vs. straight functions. In Tornado,
> the handler is a class. Not a class instance, but a class. The instance is
> created for each incoming request. Additional overhead is that the class
> supports HTTP GET by implementing `get()`. For each request, the class is
> checked to see if it has implemented the function that would exist for the
> incoming HTTP method. HTTP POST = `post()`, etc.
>
> We implemented the test with a straight-up hello world test. A very basic
> request kept the differences between using functions and classes minimal.
> The results was that functions would handle about 133 requests for every
> 100 the classes could do.
>
> Further, the reason I first liked Schematics being as instance free as
> possible, is that I could use Schematics models for building API endpoints
> and could do so without instantiating any models. No handler classes and as
> few Model classes as required to get the metaprogramming to work. For this
> case, the dict was often API inputs and I'd validate / transform this data
> based on what model the handler was built around.
>
> It's been such a long time since I used Brubeck... I think I might've put
> the results on my tumblr, which no longer exists.
>
> Accessing plain dictionaries is obviously faster, but in the context of
>> Schematics this only accounts for a fraction of the CPU costs. And any such
>> performance difference could be optimized away by making the transformation
>> loops aware of instances and having them access _data directly.
>>
> Anyone can put Schematics in a loop though.
>
>> When importing, the resulting (internal) data structure is a
>> newly-populated dictionary in any case. So literally the only difference
>> between returning a dictionary vs. a model instance is the creation of the
>> model object, which doesn't seem very expensive in the grand scheme of
>> things.
>>
>> Hence, it's my belief that, if there are any notable performance gaps
>> between plain dictionaries and model instances, they are merely design
>> issues that can be fixed.
>>
>> One scenario where, historically, there has been a significant
>> performance penalty is basic validation. Passing a dict to validate() is
>> efficient because both conversion and validation are performed at once:
>>
>> validate(MyModel, inputdict)
>>
>> Doing the same thing using an instance involves importing first and
>> validating after:
>>
>> MyModel(inputdict).validate()
>>
>> Since validation also has the conversion step baked in, the whole import
>> process is run twice for no reason.
>>
>> This is addressed in PR #374
>> <https://github.com/schematics/schematics/pull/374>, which improves the
>> performance of the above scenario by ~40%. The functional variant is now
>> only about 3% faster than creating and validating instances.
>>
> This is good news.
>
> Memory consumption is another consideration when using instances. Python
> classes are built on top of dict's, which all must have considerations on
> top for growing memory structures, and all must have unique allocations.
>
> Class instances, by nature, require more resources.
>
> Alas, Python isn't the fastest language out there.
>

On Mon, Dec 28, 2015 at 10:43 AM, James Dennis <jdennis@gmail.com> wrote:

> Do we actually need Model to implement the entire mapping interface? If
>> not, these methods could just as well be named _keys, _items, and so on.
>>
> This comes do my appreciation of not needing model instances. I think we
> should adhere to this going forward.
>
> Objects waste resources and CPU relative to just using dictionaries, so
> the purpose of objects implementing the mapping interface is so people can
> write functions that work on dict's, and if you happen to have an instance
> instead, you can use the same function.
>
>
>> One annoyance with emulating a mapping is that the keys() method is
>> unavoidably invoked by the dict constructor if you attempt naive
>> dictionary conversion via dict(instance). Right now, this effectively
>> returns a copy of instance._data — unless the model happens to have a
>> field called keys (which isn't expressly forbidden), in which case the
>> statement fails with TypeError. This occurs because dict doesn't have
>> any fallback logic in case keys isn't callable.
>>
>> Since dict(instance) is not guaranteed to work and doesn't handle
>> submodels (there's to_dict() for this reason), I'd actually prefer the
>> statement to *never* work to avoid surprises. This can be done by
>> defining a dummy class attribute called keys that would be masked by a
>> field with the same name, should it exist.
>>
> I'm fine with an error happening when someone uses a field name `keys`.
>
> Schematics blurs the line between dictionaries and models on purpose and I
> want this to stay in the system.
>
> I know I've said it before, but objects are bad. It is my view that we
> should push people towards using dicts whenever possible and avoid using
> instances of Schematics Models unless necessary. Just pipe data through the
> models, get the output you need, and move on to the next thing. For
> example, if someone needs `keys` to be part of the data, it's easy enough
> to change that by adjusting the name of the key used during serialization
> only.
>
> Personal anecdote: The difference between objects and raw functions / data
> is significant. In my days of building Brubeck (the web framework), the
> difference between using objects or functions for routing turned out to be
> roughly 33% gains when using just functions. Python is not a fast language,
> but this is significant.
>
> I understand that others in the Schematics community don't have the same
> aversion to objects that I have, but it's important to me that this is
> continued as a value. It keeps us fast and keeps our code clean and
> portable.
>

So derivatives would be float even when primals are real.

One more thing - sometimes one of arguments is required in path. For example,
```python
    def disable_rhrepo(self, base_arch,
                       release_ver, reposet_id, synchronous=True):
```
`reposet_id` from here is used in request path later. I can either 
a) leave reposet_id required:
```python
    def disable_rhrepo(self, reposet_id, payload, synchronous=True):
```
but it's move away from the style.

or
b) move everything to payload, and raise an exception if `reposet_id` wasn't found in it:
```python
    def disable_rhrepo(self, payload, synchronous=True):
        if 'reposet_id' not in payload:
            raise(...)
```
but then we're loosing following benefits:
>No changes are required if the set of arguments changes. No changes are required if the semantics of arguments changes, such as which are required or optional. This helps.
>Clients may immediately use new features without any changes on our part. This helps.

In addition `reposet_id` shouldn't actually be in payload (not a big deal, can `pop()` it out, but that looks like workaround for me).
Any suggestions/better ideas?

By default, the `self.schedule.step()` inside the model step function goes through each breed type. To turn this off run `self.schedule.step(by_breed=False)` and this will randomly go through all agents of any type.

Currently all the grass agents move, then all the sheeps and then all the wolves for example, actual activation order will vary as defaultdict is unordered. 
If you want all the wolves to go first, try reverse ordering the dictionary in the schedule step function or you can call the `self.schedule.step_breed(Wolf)` in the model step function directly.

I'm not sure what you mean by doing something with them. The step function within the agents.py is where the individual action of each wolf is coded, i.e. random step. If you meant performing some action after all the wolves have stepped, then that would be best placed in the model step function.

It looks like this bug was introduced in our last release. It will be fixed in our next release, but in meantime you can use the non-interactive option to ensure the client won't block on user input. From the output of `letsencrypt --help all`:

```
  -n, --non-interactive, --noninteractive
                        Run without ever asking for user input. This may
                        require additional command line flags; the client will
                        try to explain which ones are required if it finds one
                        missing (default: False)
```

If you set this flag on the command line or in your config file, the client will not prompt and you'll see your expected behavior.

Ok indeed there was a severe performance bug. I'm not sure I understand why it happened, but I found a fix. Loompy used to append sparse matrices 64 columns at a time. For a 480 MB test dataset this would take 1 hour and 14 minutes and result in a 3.22 GB (!) output file with exactly the same content as the original. Somehow HDF5 is wasting a lot of space.

Changing the code to write 6400 columns at a time reduced the time to 2 min 5 s and the file size to 338 MB (smaller than the input!).

Evidently there's some weird interaction between write patterns and the efficiency of compression and wasted file space. 

Test code for a dataset X of shape (27998, 65179) in COO format (`float32` elements):

```python
with loompy.connect("/Users/sten/build_20171205/L1_Medulla.loom") as ds:
    X = ds.sparse()  # 3 min 51 s
    loompy.create("test6.loom", X, ds.ra, ds.ca) # 2 min 5 s
```

Writing the matrix as `int16` reduced the time further to 1 min 45 s and the size to 280 MB:

```python
Y = sparse.coo_matrix((X.data.astype('int16'), (X.row, X.col)), shape=X.shape, dtype='int16')
with loompy.connect("/Users/sten/build_20171205/L1_Medulla.loom") as ds:
    loompy.create("test8.loom", Y, ds.ra, ds.ca)
```

I will prepare a release with this fix. Thanks for finding the bug!

I tried word embedding, it speeds up the training significantly. By using the same learning rate, to converge to the same in sample and out of sample benchmark, word embedding one takes about only 1/4 or 1/3 training epochs compared with that w/o providing features. However, the final result as long as it converges looks similar. I think lightfm indeed performs well to learn embedding itself. All these make sense to me.

BTW, I have a question, does lightfm have an api (or we can easily get it) for this task which I think is natural? Given a user that is involved in the training (basically the model has his/her latent embedding), if we have a new item that is not seen by the model before, but we know its features just as those in the training data (for example, I have its word embedding that is trained in the same way as other items), how can I predict the affinity score of this item to this user? For an already-seen item, we can use model.predict(uid, item_id) since both are registered. For this particular problem,  we have user_latent_embedding and of course we can derive item_latent_embedding, my question is: is the "score" in the lightfm just the inner product between user and item embeddings? Does lightfm have some convenient way to accomplish this task?

When `auto-config` is enabled, Z3 will enable MBQI when it sees a quantifier, so definitely disable it if you need to manually configure options. 

In this particular case however, MBQI is not the problem, instead e-matching simply starts producing lots of quantifier instances that clog the solver (it produces more and more deeply nested applications of `int_apply`). While not always possible, we can fix this particular example by adding a `no-pattern` annotation to the first quantifier, which tells Z3 not to instantiate this type of term, for instance like so:

```
(assert 
	(forall ((f Int) (g Int) (x Int)) 
        (!
            (= (int_apply (int_apply (int_apply compose f) g) x) 
                (int_apply f (int_apply g x)))
            :no-pattern (int_apply f (int_apply g x))
            ))
    )
```

This produces the desired result quickly, regardless of whether MBQI is on or off. (For more info on (no-)patterns see the [quantifier section of the tutorial](http://rise4fun.com/Z3/tutorialcontent/guide#h28).)

The (draft?) semantics has the "int.to.str" of a negative number is the empty string.
Thus, we have the axiom 

       (forall ((x Int)) (=> (< x 0) (= "" (int.to.str x)))

In previous oversions, Z3 would convert negative numbers to a signed string representation. 
As you can observe, several bugs were introduced in Z3 when adapting the semantics where
negative numbers don't produce strings. 

Quoting from the draft spec that Cesare prepared very carefully a while ago (the name str.from-int was later changed to int.to.str):

;---------------------------
; Maps to and from integers
;---------------------------

 :fun (
  ; Digit check
  ; (is-digit s) is true iff s has length 1 and its character is a decimal digit.
  ; Total
  (is-digit String Bool)

  ; Conversion to integers.
  ; (str.to-int s) with s consisting of digits is the corresponding natural number.
  ; Otherwise, it is -1. 
  ; Total
  (str.to-int String Int)

  ; Conversion from integers.
  ; (str.from-int n) with n non-negative is the corresponding string in decimal
  ; notation. Otherwise, it is the empty string. 
  ; Total
  (str.from-int Int String)
 )

> My new suggestion is to remove the Theano requirement from `setup.py`, and add a check on import that Theano is installed and `theano.__version__ >= KNOWN_GOOD_VERSION`, raising a warning with instructions to install from github if not.

Would be fine by me as a pragmatic solution, but I'd like to hear @dnouri's thoughts.

> And I'm sure that statistics will not be updated during testing because deterministic=True in this case and another piece of code will be executed.

If you call it with deterministic=False once, then the default_updates attribute would be set on the shared variables *permanently*, so calling it with deterministic=True afterwards (within the same script) would still include the updates. That's why I had to clone the shared variables instead of using the original ones.

> cell is the top of the network inside scan? What does the dictionary mean? I don't see l_inp being defined anywhere?

Yes cell is the top. The dictionary just maps a layer to be iterated over to a cell input. I've edited the above post, `l_inp` is just any layer to be iterated over.

> I guess this covers RNN autoencoders where the last hidden state of the encoder is used as input at every timestep for the decoder?

I think so, but I'm not 100% sure as I haven't implemented a text autoencoder yet. Same goes with attention. I think the soft attention in "Show, Attend and Tell: Neural Image Caption Generation with Visual Attention, Kelvin Xu et al." can be made into a layer, as it's just re-weighting the same vectors {a}_i in each time step using a function of the previous lstm hidden state and {a}_i.

But that does suggest a problem with the current api, I need a way to feed the output at t-1 into a different cell's input at time t. Take a dummy example, say I want to feed the output of a lstm's into the next time step's input. Here's one solution I thought of:

    cell_inp_1 = lasagne.layers.InputLayer((num_batch, n_features))
    cell_inp_2 = lasagne.layers.InputLayer((num_batch, n_features))
    cell = ConcatLayer(cell_inp_1, cell_inp_2)
    cell = LSTMCell(cell_inp_1, num_units_2)['output']
    l_rec_2 = lasagne.layers.RecurrentContainerLayer({cell_inp_1: l_inp}, {cell_inp_2: cell}, cell)

Here `cell_inp_1` is the layer input, and `cell_inp_2` is the lstm's output. `{cell_inp_1: l_inp}` specifies which sequence we are feeding into which cell. `{cell_inp_2: cell}` specifies which cell's previous state we are feeding into which cell.

> Combining an RNN with the spatial transformer layer and feed the image glimpse at timestep t-1 into the RNN at timestep t? 

Which paper is this? I haven't read about spatial transformer networks yet.

> btw does our code handle randomness inside scan?

There can be a dropout mask that is unchanged throughout inside scan. Is this what you mean?

Just to be clear, I'll answer initial questions:

> 1.- The readme says:

> > The formatting is turned on if you have set USE_L10N=True in the your settings file.
> > If formatting is disabled in the configuration, then in the templates will be used default formatting.
> > In the templates you can use a special tag to format the money.

> this means that if USE_L10N=True I do not need {% money_localize money_object %} and i can just > use {{ money_object }} or this means that {% money_localize money_object %} works only if USE_L10N=True??

Localization behaviour depends on ``py-moneyed`` settings. For example we have ``LANGUAGE_CODE='pl', ``USE_L10N=True`` and formatting definition as the following (taken from ``tests/settings.py``):
```
from decimal import ROUND_HALF_EVEN
import moneyed
from moneyed.localization import _FORMATTER, DEFAULT

_FORMATTER.add_sign_definition('pl_PL', moneyed.PLN, suffix=' zł')
_FORMATTER.add_sign_definition(DEFAULT, moneyed.PLN, suffix=' zł')
_FORMATTER.add_formatting_definition(
    'pl_PL', group_size=3, group_separator=' ', decimal_point=',',
    positive_sign='', trailing_positive_sign='',
    negative_sign='-', trailing_negative_sign='',
    rounding_method=ROUND_HALF_EVEN
)
```

Lets render a few templates with a simple context - ``{'money': Money('2.3', 'PLN')}``:
```
{{ money }}
{% load djmoney %}{% money_localize money %}
{% load djmoney %}{% money_localize money on %}
```

Output:
```
2,30 zł
2,30 zł
2,30 zł
```
With ``USE_L10N=False``:
```
2.30 zł
2,30 zł
2,30 zł
```
Here in the first line we have '.' instead of ',' - it is because the value was rendered without explicitly specified locale and ``py-moneyed`` used ``DEFAULT`` locale for formatting. This locale has the following [definition](https://github.com/limist/py-moneyed/blob/master/moneyed/localization.py#L132) (it has '.' as a decimal point).
So, basically the tag adds an ability to force usage of specified locale in money formatting.

> 2.- Whats the correct way of handling empty values in forms?. Originally I wanted to default to None but aparently this is not possible so my default value es 0. If a user sends and empty amount field should I change it to 0 in the view?

If you'll add ``blank=True`` to your field with default values and everything should work fine. I'll update the answer later with more info

> 3.- My money field defines decimal_places=0 and I want to print the money field in the templates without the trailing .00 (ej: CLP$100,002.00 -> CLP$100,002), do you know a way for doing this?

You can use ``CURRENCY_DECIMAL_PLACES`` setting to control this.

> 4.- I'm not sure if the localization is working for me: {% money_localize money_object %} prints CLP$100,002.00 but in my country (Chile) the thousands separator is '.' and the decimal separator is ',' (so it should print CLP$100.002,00)

To make it work you have to define the following in your settings:
```

from moneyed.localization import _FORMATTER, DEFAULT

_FORMATTER.add_sign_definition('es_CL', moneyed.CLP, prefix='CLP$')

    positive_sign='', trailing_positive_sign='',
    negative_sign='-', trailing_negative_sign='',
    rounding_method=ROUND_HALF_EVEN
)

*  We want  a heap state-plugin that provides an interface for allocator actions as `malloc`, `free`, etc.
* The plugin keeps track of all the heap metadata and states
* The plugin should behave according to some real world implementation, therefore:
     * Have a generic plugin that that provides access to `brk` and `mmap`
     * Have a specific plugin for e.g. `DLMallocHeap`, `PTMallocHeap` that mimics the particular implementation 
     * Each plugin provides an api for `.malloc()`, `.free()`, which can be used by the particular simprocedure

In that case, I agree with @zardus, it makes sense to have a plugin for each allocator's implementation.

We don’t automatically endian-correct for memory loads, because it’s hard
to tell whether or not the data _should_ be endian-corrected (for example,
if you are reading a string from memory, it should not be endian-corrected).

You can make angr correct the endianness by doing `s.memory.load(s.regs.ebp
+ 8, endness=“Iend_LE”)`.

Note to the dev team: maybe we should make angr endian-correct when a size
is not specified for the load (as in this example)? In that case, you’re
intentionally grabbing “data of the architecture width” rather than “data
of a width that might happen to match the architecture width”, and I feel
that it’s reasonable to, in that case, expect that the data will be
endian-corrected. Thoughts?

I figured out something more advanced: receiving group chat messages.

In steam/core/msg.py, in the Msg class constructor, I added this condition to the chain creating the appropriate message body:

elif msg == EMsg.ClientChatMsg:
	    self.body = ClientChatMsg(data)

ClientChatMsg is a class I created in the same file after looking at the format in which steam sends group chat messages. This is basically: 

steamIdChatter - 64-bit ID  of the author of the message
steamIdChatRoom - 64 bit ID of the chatroom the message was sent in
ChatMsgType - 32-bit int message type
ChatMsg - the message itself. Size is as big as needed.

This can be unpacked with struct.unpack_from with the format string "<QQI16s", similarly to other messages, although this particular string will only unpack the first 16 characters correctly, not sure how to make it unpack everything until the end of the message.

Once we have this unpacked, we set a ClientChatMsg object as the message body and voila. The last piece of the puzzle is figuring out the format string for struct.unpack_from, and I can post a pull request.

"imo the api should accept timeout value in minutes only as the junos rpc takes the value of timeout in minutes."
@ganeshrn the problem with this is that the ncclient library (and netconf) uses timeout in seconds, so if you don't provide seconds there, you break the API of ncclient library. The standard rpc for confirmed commit does not seem to work for Junos, so we are forced to wrap their proprietary and incompatible rpc to the standard API of ncclient, and convert the seconds to minutes in the best possible way.
IMHO, for practical purposes, one minute difference does not matter much for confirmed commit. Realistically, the timeout should be at least 10 minutes (default value in the protocol) to leave enough time for testing after configuration. But I am not a network engineer, do not have practical experience here. And it is better to leave one extra minute for the testing rather than timeout a bit earlier. To put it another way (this is the only thing I can imagine to support your opinion): in case of a totally bad config one might generate one extra minute of outage.
In any case thank you very much helping to fix this! :)
